event: The Neolithic Cucuteni-Trypillia culture flourishes across present-day Ukraine, building some of the largest known settlements of its era.
Communities on the Pontic-Caspian steppe domesticate the horse, a development central to the spread of Indo-European peoples.
Scythian nomads establish dominance over the steppe lands north of the Black Sea.
Greek colonists found Black Sea trading cities such as Olbia and later Chersonesus and Panticapaeum.
The Bosporan Kingdom emerges around the Kerch Strait, linking Greek and indigenous cultures.
Gothic and later Hunnic migrations sweep across the region, reshaping the steppe peoples.
Avar and then Khazar power dominates the steppe, with the Khazar Khaganate controlling trade routes.
Prince Oleh seizes Kyiv and makes it the capital of the emerging Kyivan Rus state.
Grand Prince Volodymyr the Great adopts Christianity, baptizing Kyivan Rus into the Eastern Orthodox faith.
Yaroslav the Wise begins his reign, ushering in a golden age of law, learning, and church-building in Kyiv.
The name 'Ukraina' is first recorded in the Hypatian Codex chronicle.
Mongol forces under Batu Khan sack Kyiv, ending the dominance of Kyivan Rus.
Danylo of Halych is crowned King of Ruthenia, consolidating the Kingdom of Galicia-Volhynia.
The Grand Duchy of Lithuania defeats the Golden Horde and absorbs much of central Ukraine including Kyiv.
The Union of Lublin transfers most Ukrainian lands from Lithuania to the Kingdom of Poland.
The Union of Brest creates the Greek Catholic (Uniate) Church, splitting Ukrainian religious allegiance.
Bohdan Khmelnytsky leads a Cossack uprising against Polish rule, founding the Cossack Hetmanate.
The Treaty of Pereyaslav places the Cossack Hetmanate under the protection of the Russian Tsar.
Hetman Ivan Mazepa's alliance with Sweden ends in defeat at the Battle of Poltava, tightening Russian control.
Catherine the Great abolishes the office of Hetman, dismantling Cossack autonomy.
Russian forces destroy the Zaporozhian Sich, ending the Cossack stronghold's independence.
The Second Partition of Poland brings much of right-bank Ukraine under Russian Imperial rule.
Taras Shevchenko publishes Kobzar, igniting a Ukrainian national and literary revival.
The Ems Ukaz bans the use of the Ukrainian language in print and public life within the Russian Empire.
The Central Rada proclaims the Ukrainian People's Republic amid the Russian Revolution.
Ukraine declares full independence as rival governments and foreign armies vie for control.
The Treaty of Riga partitions Ukraine between Soviet Russia and Poland, ending the independence struggle.
The Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic becomes a founding member of the Soviet Union.
Stalin's forced collectivization triggers the Holodomor, a man-made famine that kills millions of Ukrainians.
Nazi Germany invades Soviet Ukraine, beginning a brutal occupation and the mass murder of Ukrainian Jews.
The Soviet Union secures western Ukrainian lands, and the Ukrainian SSR becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
The Crimean Peninsula is transferred from the Russian SFSR to the Ukrainian SSR.
The Chernobyl nuclear reactor explodes, causing history's worst nuclear accident and spreading radiation across Europe.
Ukraine declares independence from the Soviet Union, confirmed by a national referendum in December.
Ukraine signs the Budapest Memorandum, giving up its nuclear arsenal in exchange for security assurances.
Ukraine adopts a new constitution and introduces the hryvnia as its national currency.
The Orange Revolution overturns a rigged presidential election after mass peaceful protests.
The Euromaidan Revolution of Dignity ousts President Yanukovych; Russia annexes Crimea and foments war in the Donbas.
Ukraine secures visa-free travel to the European Union and brings its Association Agreement into force.
Volodymyr Zelensky is elected president in a landslide victory.
Russia launches a full-scale invasion of Ukraine, triggering Europe's largest war since 1945; Ukraine is granted EU candidate status.
Ukraine begins formal accession negotiations with the European Union while continuing to resist the Russian invasion.
